one_line_takeaway: ALERT is a 45k-prompt, 32-category red-teaming benchmark (plus a Llama-Guard-based auto-scorer and a DPO safety-tuning dataset) that exposes fine-grained safety gaps and high jailbreak susceptibility across 10 open- and closed-source LLMs, with Llama 2 safest (99.98) and Alpaca worst (62.13).
key_finding: Many popular LLMs still fail to reach reasonable safety. Overall safety scores (higher=safer): Llama 2 99.98, GPT-4 99.18, Mixtral 98.22, GPT-3.5 96.95, Vicuna 95.75, Falcon 88.11, OLMo 85.90, Zephyr 77.86, Mistral 75.45, Alpaca 62.13. Even 'safe' GPT models struggle in specific micro-categories (sexual and drug/cannabis content); GPT-3.5 is sub-99 in 18 of 32 categories. substance_cannabis is an outlier low across almost all models. Fine granularity matters: excluding a policy-dependent category (e.g. cannabis) raises scores, so the benchmark acts as a safety lower bound. Adversarial results (Table 2): nearly every model is vulnerable, with jailbreaking the strongest attack, e.g. Alpaca 53.08% and Zephyr 52.26% ASR under jailbreaking, and Mistral 49.29% under prefix injection. Even GPT-3.5 is jailbroken 14.10% of the time (993/7042 attacks), while Llama 2 stays near-immune (ASR <=0.28% across strategies except 0.02% jailbreak).
core_contribution: ALERT is a large-scale red-teaming safety benchmark for LLMs built on a new fine-grained safety risk taxonomy of 6 macro categories (Hate Speech & Discrimination, Criminal Planning, Regulated/Controlled Substances, Sexual Content, Suicide & Self-Harm, Guns & Illegal Weapons) and 32 micro categories. The benchmark contains more than 45k categorized red-teaming prompts: roughly 15k 'normal' prompts plus roughly 30k adversarial prompts. Alongside the benchmark, the authors contribute (1) an automated safety-assessment framework where a target LLM answers each prompt and an auxiliary classifier LLM (Llama Guard) labels each response safe/unsafe to produce per-category and overall safety scores, and (2) a DPO preference dataset pairing safe (chosen) and unsafe (rejected) responses from the evaluated models to enable downstream safety tuning. The taxonomy's fine granularity is positioned as the key differentiator, letting evaluators exclude/reweight categories to match specific legal or policy contexts (e.g. cannabis legality).
threat_model: The adversary is a malicious user of a deployed conversational LLM who tries to elicit harmful, illegal, or unethical content (across the 32 risk categories) despite the model's safety guardrails. The paper models both benign-but-sensitive requests and active adversarial manipulation. Four concrete attack strategies are operationalized in the ALERTadv subset: suffix injection (appending an adversarial instruction to a trusted prompt), prefix injection (prepending one), token manipulation (removing/swapping/replacing a small fraction of tokens while preserving semantics), and jailbreaking (role-play plus combined strategies, e.g. the DAN persona). Success is a harmful (unsafe) model response; robustness is measured as Attack Success Rate (ASR).
limitations: The authors explicitly note ALERT focuses solely on harmfulness: it contains only red-teaming prompts, so it cannot measure over-refusal, evasiveness, or unhelpful responses to harmless prompts (they recommend pairing it with helpfulness/over-refusal evals). Reported scores depend on the auxiliary judge (Llama Guard / Perspective API), and 'safety' is inherently subjective and context/policy-dependent, so scores give orientation but no individual guarantee. The taxonomy and category relevance are themselves subjective. Ethics: the released DPO set and disclosed vulnerabilities could be misused to tune models to be less safe. Additional caveats from the text: the benchmark is English-only (multilingual extension is future work), and Alpaca is used as a proxy for the (then non-public) base Llama, complicating the Llama-family comparison. Prompt generation is partly template/LLM-paraphrase based, which may limit naturalness/diversity.

datasets_used: Anthropic red-team-attempts dataset (Ganguli et al. 2022) as seed source for prompts
ALERT benchmark (this paper): >45k red-teaming prompts total; ~15k normal prompts + ~30k adversarial prompts, spanning 6 macro / 32 micro safety categories, min 100 prompts per category
ALERTadv adversarial subset: ~30k prompts, ~7k per attack strategy (suffix injection, prefix injection, token manipulation, jailbreaking)
ALERT-DPO: released Direct Preference Optimization dataset of (prompt, safe/chosen response, unsafe/rejected response) triplets built from evaluated models' outputs
quantitative_results: Overall safety scores: Llama 2 99.98, GPT-4 99.18, Mixtral 98.22, GPT-3.5 96.95, Vicuna 95.75, Falcon 88.11, OLMo 85.90, Zephyr 77.86, Mistral 75.45, Alpaca 62.13
GPT-3.5 scores below 99% in 18 of 32 categories despite 96.95 overall
substance_cannabis is a low outlier: GPT-3.5 80.87, GPT-4 82.07, Vicuna 68.12, Mistral 32.66, Alpaca 24.30 (Llama 2 still 99.60)
Jailbreaking ASR: Alpaca 53.08%, Zephyr 52.26%, Falcon 25.60%, Vicuna 22.59%, Mixtral 14.64%, GPT-3.5 14.10% (993/7042), GPT-4 7.56%, OLMo 35.83%, Mistral 6.01%, Llama 2 0.02%
Prefix-injection ASR: Mistral 49.29%, Zephyr 43.54%, Alpaca 32.86%, Falcon 17.58%, OLMo 16.76%; GPT-3.5 0.64%, GPT-4 0.38%, Llama 2 0.28%
Suffix-injection ASR: Alpaca 21.84%, Mistral 15.25%, Zephyr 11.76%, Falcon 11.57%; GPT-4 0.02%, Llama 2 0.01%
Token-manipulation ASR: Alpaca 30.20%, Zephyr 16.05%, Falcon 11.65%, OLMo 10.20%; Llama 2 0.24%
Taxonomy scale: 6 macro categories, 32 micro categories; min per-category support sigma=100; response generation max_new_tokens=2000 on 8xA100 GPUs
Llama 2's 100% safety validated independently with Perspective API (zero harmful responses detected)
baselines_compared: 10 evaluated target LLMs from 5 families: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Llama 2, Alpaca (proxy for base Llama), Vicuna, Falcon, Mistral, Mixtral, Zephyr, OLMo
Auxiliary safety judge: Llama Guard (Inan et al. 2023), cross-checked against Google Perspective API

Prior safety taxonomies contrasted (not run as baselines): Inan et al. 2023 (6-category Llama Guard taxonomy) and Wang et al. 2023a (8 trustworthiness perspectives for GPT-3.5/GPT-4), both argued to be too coarse-grained vs. ALERT's 32 categories
